---
title: "Simulation Fidelity and Physical Constraints"
chapter_id: "simulation-fidelity-and-physical-constraints"
part_id: "routing-compression-representation-substrates"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-27"
primary_sources:
  - "simulation_scaling"
  - "cgs"
  - "rankfold_neuralfold"
  - "tokenmana"
  - "alignment_field"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Appendix C now records exact passage-reviewed mappings for all five assigned simulation-fidelity sources; support remains at argument until a physical-computation audit, simulation benchmark, feasibility calculator, independent external literature review, or accepted evidence transition exists."
  - "The simulation_contract_record fixture validates schema/example consistency for contract version, claim class, fidelity state, assumptions, omitted variables, resource bill, instrumentation effects, transfer decision, support-state effect, failure behavior, and non-claims only; no physical-computation audit, simulation benchmark, feasibility calculator, resource-bound sanity check, approximation audit, or external literature review has been run."
  - "Simulation Scaling is treated as internal theoretical synthesis and design framing, not as reproduced physics evidence or an independent literature audit."
  - "Alignment Field is used only for normative/speculative boundary handling around simulation scenarios; it is not physics or simulation evidence, and consciousness/metaphysical claims remain speculative."
  - "No support-state promotion, raw source publication, physical feasibility result, simulator result, or benchmark-transfer result is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `simulation-fidelity-and-physical-constraints` |
| Part | Part III - Routing, Compression, Representation, and Substrates |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-27 |
| Primary source records | `simulation_scaling`, `cgs`, `rankfold_neuralfold`, `tokenmana`, `alignment_field` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `simulation_scaling`; supporting: `cgs`, `rankfold_neuralfold`, `tokenmana`, `alignment_field` |
| Source loading state | source notes and passage-reviewed local raw-cache mappings exist for `simulation_scaling`, `cgs`, `rankfold_neuralfold`, `tokenmana`, `alignment_field`; raw source text is not published. |
| Test state | `simulation_contract_record.valid.json` passes repository-level protocol fixture validation for contract version, claim class, declared fidelity state, assumptions, omitted variables, resource bill, instrumentation effects, transfer decision, support-state effect, failure behavior, and non-claims; `AsiStackProofs.SimulationFidelity` implements finite claim-field and fidelity-support predicates; fidelity, resource-bound, approximation-audit, and feasibility tests remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter treats simulation claims as contract-relative design constraints. It does not make new physics claims, reproduce physical-computation literature, or claim that any simulation benchmark has been run in this repository.

This is the closing realism check for the preceding resource-economics chapter. Resource budgets say what the stack can afford; simulation contracts say what an experiment is actually allowed to mean. The handoff matters because a cheap synthetic world can be useful for a narrow invariant while still being too abstract, too slow, too lossy, or too physically expensive for a stronger claim.

The central object is not the simulator; it is the claim transport rule. A result may travel from simulated world to book claim only along the variables, approximations, clocks, resources, and omissions named in the contract.

::: {.asi-human-only}
## Human Reading Path

Resource budgets say what the stack can afford. Simulation fidelity says what a cheap or synthetic experiment is allowed to mean. This chapter prevents simulated success from being treated as stronger evidence than its model, scope, and physical assumptions support.

The practical standard is contract-relative realism. A simulation can be useful, but it should say which world it models, which constraints it omits, which resources it assumes, which claims it can support, and which deployment questions remain untouched.
:::

## Problem

ASI discussions can make simulations sound unlimited: nested worlds, accelerated training universes, perfect sandboxes, exhaustive synthetic evaluation, or arbitrary copies of physical systems. Simulation Scaling Law pushes the book toward a stricter question: what scope, clockspeed, fidelity, output contract, efficiency, and capacity are being claimed?

In the ASI Stack, simulations are useful only if their contract is visible. A toy simulator can support a narrow unit test. A reduced-fidelity environment can support a benchmark with stated approximations. A high-fidelity world simulation must account for compute, memory, heat, bandwidth, latency, and temporal semantics.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Simulation-based claims can become unbounded if scope, fidelity, resource cost, and observer effects are not explicit. Theoretical computability does not imply physical feasibility. A simulation that is useful for one invariant can be misleading for another. A benchmark environment can be convenient while omitting the very constraints a claim depends on.

Resource economics matters here because simulation consumes capacity. Compact generative systems matter because a compact simulator can hide residual complexity. Artifact compression matters because storing traces and states can become part of the cost. Alignment scenarios matter because speculative metaphysics should not be smuggled into engineering claims.

The most common error is letting a simulation inherit the authority of the thing it imitates. A simulator may preserve a causal structure, a statistical pattern, a control interface, or a failure surface, but rarely all of them at once. A result that is valid for a toy dynamic may not transfer to a physical device. A result that is valid for a narrow benchmark environment may not transfer to an open world. A result that is useful for stress-testing a policy may not justify claims about consciousness, moral status, or confinement.

The contract prevents that slippage by making simulation a translation problem. What is translated from the target system into the simulated world? What is omitted? What is distorted by time scale, resolution, resource ceilings, or observation? What part of the result is allowed to travel back? A simulation can be extremely valuable when those questions are answered, but it should not receive a blank check simply because it looks like an environment.

Fidelity is therefore relative to the intended claim. A low-fidelity simulator can be adequate for a lifecycle transition test and inadequate for a physical-deployment claim. A high-fidelity simulator can be impressive and still irrelevant if it omits the bottleneck that decides the question.

## Core Claim

[simulation-fidelity-and-physical-constraints.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Simulation and fidelity claims should be bounded by resource constraints and used as design constraints rather than assumed unlimited substrates.

The claim remains at `argument` support. The passage-reviewed mappings support discussion of contract-relative simulation demand and bottlenecks, but this chapter does not claim independent physical validation, feasibility calculation, or simulation performance.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C now records passage-reviewed mappings for all five assigned sources. The mappings support contract-relative scope, fidelity, resource costs, bottlenecks, hidden simulation burden, trace/storage cost, load pressure, and normative/speculative boundary handling, not a demonstrated simulator, physical-computation result, benchmark-transfer result, or consciousness claim. `alignment_field` is used only as a guardrail around agency, dignity, confinement, and speculative metaphysical assumptions.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `simulation_scaling` | Contract-relative feasibility through scope, clockspeed, fidelity, temporal semantics, demand estimates, available capacity, and compute/memory/heat/bandwidth/latency bottlenecks. | Theoretical synthesis only; no physical experiment, simulation benchmark, feasibility calculator, or independent literature audit was run. |
| `cgs` | Warning that compact simulators and generators can hide residual complexity unless residual burden, verification cost, governance interface, and hidden complexity debt are explicit. | Conceptual framework only; no local CGS benchmark or simulator adequacy test has been run. |
| `rankfold_neuralfold` | Artifact manifests, deterministic reconstruction checks, residual coding, WORM assumptions, and archive-backend obligations around simulation and trace storage. | No local artifact compressor, trace-store benchmark, deterministic decoder, or reproduced ratio exists here. |
| `tokenmana` | Simulation as a scarce-resource choice through bounded capacity, burst controls, load variance, cognitive friction, and quality/cost measurement ideas. | No TokenMana simulation, pricing experiment, human study, or local load-stability evidence exists. |
| `alignment_field` | Normative/speculative boundaries around agency, dignity, confinement, consciousness heuristics, and metaphysical assumptions in simulation scenarios. | Philosophical/normative source, not physics or simulation evidence; consciousness and metaphysical claims remain speculative. |

## Mechanism

Simulation is a substrate only after it becomes an instrument with a declared contract. `simulation_scaling` supplies the feasibility frame: scope, clockspeed, fidelity, temporal semantics, efficiency, and bottlenecks have to be named before a result can mean anything. CGS and RankFold/NeuralFold add the hidden-burden warning: a compact simulator or stored trace can hide complexity in residuals, manifests, reconstruction checks, or storage costs. TokenMana adds the budget pressure, and `alignment_field` keeps speculative agency and metaphysical claims from becoming engineering evidence.

A Simulation Contract Record makes that boundary inspectable. It records the simulated target, contract version, claim class, fidelity promise, assumptions, omitted variables, resource bill, approximation liberties, instrumentation effects, transfer decision, failure behavior, support-state effect, non-claims, and the exact claim boundary the simulation is allowed to support.

The record also separates three uses that are often conflated. A simulation can be a unit-test surface for a local invariant, a benchmark surface for comparing systems under declared approximations, or a scenario surface for exploring possible risks. These uses have different evidential weight. Unit tests can check a narrow transition. Benchmarks can compare behavior inside the environment. Scenarios can organize imagination and red-team thinking. None of those automatically proves physical feasibility, open-world transfer, or moral significance.

A simulation result should therefore emit a fidelity receipt. The receipt records the contract version, input assumptions, omitted variables, resource bill, observed result, claim boundary, and transfer decision. If the result is useful only as scenario evidence, the receipt should say so.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Simulation claim"] --> B["Scope"]
  B --> C["Fidelity standard"]
  C --> D["Temporal semantics"]
  D --> E["Demand estimate"]
  E --> F["Capacity bottlenecks"]
  F --> G{"Feasible for claim?"}
  G -- "yes" --> H["Bounded experiment or benchmark"]
  G -- "no" --> I["Residual / reduced scope / blocked claim"]
  H --> J["Evidence boundary"]
  I --> J
```

The record should specify:

- Scope: what system, variables, agents, or degrees of freedom are represented.
- Claim class: invariant check, benchmark comparison, feasibility estimate, scenario exploration, safety rehearsal, or speculative thought experiment.
- Fidelity standard: what accuracy or abstraction level is required.
- Fidelity state: declared, under-specified, blocked, or not applicable.
- Temporal semantics: real-time, faster-than-real-time, offline, or event-driven.
- Input assumptions and omitted variables.
- Demand estimate: compute, memory, bandwidth, storage, latency, and energy assumptions.
- Resource bill and instrumentation effects.
- Capacity bottlenecks: the limiting physical or infrastructure resources.
- Approximation liberties: simplifications, omissions, and model mismatches.
- Claim boundary: what the simulation can and cannot support.
- Transfer decision, support-state effect, failure behavior, residual risks, and non-claims.

This turns simulation from a vague substrate into a bounded experimental instrument.

When a simulation fails the contract, the right move is not necessarily to abandon it. The system can reduce scope, lower the support claim, expose a residual, add missing resource estimates, or route the result to a speculative scenario lane. The important thing is that the claim moves down to match the fidelity rather than the prose moving up to match the ambition.

The contract should also name instrumentation effects. A simulator that changes behavior through observation, logging, compression, sampling, or intervention has a fidelity cost that belongs in the result, not in a footnote after promotion.

## Interfaces

The interface is the Simulation Contract Record.

Minimum fields:

- `simulation_id`
- `claim_id`
- `contract_version`
- `claim_class`
- `scope`
- `fidelity_standard`
- `fidelity_state`
- `temporal_semantics`
- `input_assumptions`
- `demand_estimate`
- `resource_bill`
- `capacity_bottlenecks`
- `omitted_variables`
- `approximation_liberties`
- `instrumentation_effects`
- `supported_claim_boundary`
- `observed_result_boundary`
- `transfer_decision`
- `support_state_effect`
- `failure_behavior`
- `residual_risks`
- `evidence_refs`
- `non_claims`

Benchmarks record fidelity limits. Resource budgets account for simulation cost. Evidence states prevent approximate simulations from supporting claims beyond their contract. Prototype roadmaps use the record to decide whether a simulation is a useful test surface or just an attractive metaphor.

The interface should separate claim class: invariant check, benchmark comparison, feasibility estimate, scenario exploration, safety rehearsal, or speculative thought experiment. Each class has a different admissible support state.

## Invariants

- Fidelity is declared.
- Resource bounds are explicit.
- Speculative simulation metaphysics stays separated from engineering claims.
- An experiment result cannot support claims outside its fidelity contract.
- Approximation liberties remain visible after results are summarized.

The key invariant is claim-boundedness. A simulation can be useful and still be inadequate for the stronger claim someone wants to make.

Another invariant is non-transfer by default. Simulation results do not travel to broader deployment, physical feasibility, moral-status, or safety claims unless the contract explicitly licenses that transfer and records the remaining residuals.

## Failure modes

- Overclaiming nested simulation feasibility.
- Ignoring resource ceilings.
- Treating approximate simulations as ground truth.
- Changing the parent physics assumptions without recording it.
- Using a benchmark sandbox as evidence for open-world deployment.

Each failure should create a residual or downgrade. The correct response to a weak simulation is not to discard simulation; it is to reduce the claim, state the approximation, or run a better experiment.

The subtle failure is simulation laundering. A synthetic environment is used to produce a clean result, then the result is described as if the omitted variables, resource bottlenecks, and transfer limits were irrelevant.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

Simulation fidelity should begin as a simulation contract record. The repository fixture records contract version, claim class, scope, fidelity state, time semantics, assumptions, demand, resource bill, bottlenecks, omitted variables, approximations, instrumentation effects, supported claim boundary, observed-result boundary, transfer decision, support-state effect, failure behavior, residuals, evidence references, and non-claims.

Passing the fixture does not show feasibility. It makes the feasibility claim inspectable.

The next useful fixture set is a simulation receipt suite: one unit-invariant result, one benchmark result with approximation limits, one scenario-only result, one blocked transfer because a bottleneck is missing, and one downgraded claim after instrumentation cost is recorded.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature version of Simulation Fidelity and Physical Constraints is a simulation claim-transport layer. It lets the stack run synthetic worlds, unit simulators, benchmark environments, safety rehearsals, and scenario models while preventing their results from traveling farther than their scope, fidelity, resources, and omissions allow.

The operational contract is that a simulation result travels only as far as its fidelity standard, scope, assumptions, omitted variables, resource bill, and transfer decision allow. The simulation claim-transport layer carries that limit through:

- Operating mechanism: simulation contracts record claim class, scope, fidelity standard, temporal semantics, demand estimate, resource bill, capacity bottlenecks, assumptions, omitted variables, approximation liberties, instrumentation effects, supported-claim boundary, observed-result boundary, transfer decision, residual risks, and non-claims.
- Evidence surface: invariant checks, benchmark comparisons, feasibility estimates, safety rehearsals, scenario explorations, and speculative thought experiments remain distinct evidence classes with different support-state effects.
- Stack interface: resource economics prices simulation cost; benchmark ratchets preserve fidelity limits; alignment chapters keep agency and metaphysical speculation separated from engineering evidence; artifact graphs preserve simulator configs, traces, and downgrade decisions.
- Upgrade loop: failed transfers reduce claim scope, missing bottlenecks become residuals, instrumentation costs force downgrades, approximate worlds stay useful for narrow tests, and stronger simulations earn only the specific claim boundary their contract supports.
- Failure closure: nested-simulation overclaiming, ignored resource ceilings, approximate worlds treated as ground truth, parent-physics assumption drift, sandbox-to-open-world transfer, and simulation laundering become blocked transfers, downgraded support states, residual records, or scenario-only labels.

This simulation claim-transport layer remains a target architecture, not a current-result claim. Support should stay at `argument` until fidelity declarations, physical-constraint records, transfer checks, negative simulation results, and claim-boundary reviews show which lessons survive outside the simulated surface.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Simulation contract record fixture validation | Validate that a simulation claim records contract version, claim class, scope, fidelity state, temporal semantics, assumptions, demand, resource bill, bottlenecks, omissions, approximation liberties, instrumentation effects, supported and observed-result boundaries, transfer decision, support-state effect, failure behavior, residual risks, evidence references, and non-claims. | implemented; passing via `python3 scripts/validate_protocol_examples.py` |
| Fidelity declaration test | Check that every simulation claim declares scope, fidelity, and temporal semantics. | planned; not run |
| Resource-bound sanity check | Check that demand and capacity bottlenecks are named before feasibility is claimed. | planned; not run |
| Simulation approximation audit | Check that conclusions do not exceed approximation and fidelity boundaries. | planned; not run |

The implemented row validates fixture/schema consistency only. The remaining rows require simulation claims, resource estimates, and a review harness; they are not reported feasibility results. When a simulation-audit test is implemented, this chapter should link to the command, fixture, assumptions, environment notes, and result summary, and Appendix E should be regenerated or updated accordingly.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:simulation.fidelity.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.SimulationFidelity` | A simulation claim includes declared scope, fidelity, and resource bounds. | implemented |
| `lean:simulation.fidelity.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.SimulationFidelity` | An experiment result cannot exceed the declared fidelity support of its simulation. | implemented |

These Lean hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates over simulation claim fields and declared support levels. They prove only the record-level gates: a simulation claim used as evidence includes scope, fidelity, and resource bounds, and a promoted experiment result cannot exceed its declared fidelity-support level. They do not prove physical feasibility, validate external physics assumptions, run a simulator, or establish that any benchmark environment transfers to open-world deployment.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `simulation_scaling` | Simulation Scaling Law | compute_fidelity_constraints | Resource constraints on scope, clockspeed, and fidelity in simulations. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `cgs` | Compact Generative Systems | compression_representation | Smallest adequate structure that can generate/govern target without hiding residual complexity. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `rankfold_neuralfold` | RankFold + NeuralFold | compression_representation | Tensor/artifact compression. Low-rank residual coding plus functional preprocessing and probe-route fallback. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `tokenmana` | TokenMana | resource_economics | Regenerative capacity mechanisms for load-stable token pricing. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `alignment_field` | Field of God / Alignment Field family | alignment_constitution | Primary Alignment Field draft found. Use with Ethica Mechanica and Eternal Code. | source note available; local raw cache available |

The crosswalk keeps simulation language engineering-bound. `simulation_scaling` supplies contract-relative feasibility, while `cgs`, `rankfold_neuralfold`, and `tokenmana` make hidden generation, storage, and resource costs visible. `alignment_field` is included only as alignment-scenario context; it should not turn speculative simulation metaphysics into an engineering claim.

## Summary

Simulation is not a magic substrate. It is a contract with scope, fidelity, clockspeed, cost, bottlenecks, approximations, and claim boundaries.

The ASI Stack can use simulations aggressively, but only when they are treated as bounded instruments whose conclusions cannot outrun their fidelity and resource assumptions. The next chapter generalizes that rule from simulators to mathematical and search substrates: no substrate becomes core merely because it is elegant.

The practical standard is that a simulation should make the boundary of its usefulness clearer. If the result makes the claim sound broader than the contract permits, the simulation has become rhetoric rather than evidence.
